questions.prompt: Rigid motion that slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction.
questions.explanation: A translation is a rigid motion (isometry) that slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction, defined by a translation vector. The coordinate rule is T_(a,b)(x,y)=(x+a,y+b). Because all distances and angle measures are preserved, the image is congruent to the pre-image (G-CO.2, G-CO.4). Translations have no fixed points. On NY Geometry Regents, students must identify the vector from a pre-image/image pair or apply a given rule. Translations form an abelian group under composition — order does not change the result.

questions.prompt: Rigid motion that turns a figure about a fixed center through a given angle.
questions.explanation: A rotation is a rigid motion that turns every point of a figure through a fixed angle about a fixed center. The center is the only fixed point. Common Regents rotations about the origin: R_90(x,y)=(-y,x), R_180(x,y)=(-x,-y), R_270(x,y)=(y,-x). Because rotations are isometries, they preserve distance and angle measure, so image ≅ pre-image (G-CO.2, G-CO.4). Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE) treated rotation informally; Felix Klein's Erlangen Program (1872) formalized the group-theoretic view of rotations.

questions.prompt: Rigid motion that flips a figure across a line so the line is its perpendicular bisector for each point.
questions.explanation: A reflection is a rigid motion that maps every point P to P' such that the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of PP'. Points on the line are fixed. Common Regents reflections: across x-axis (x,y)→(x,-y), across y-axis →(-x,y), across y=x →(y,x). Reflections reverse orientation — a clockwise figure becomes counterclockwise (G-CO.2, G-CO.4). Two reflections across intersecting lines produce a rotation; across parallel lines, a translation.

questions.prompt: Composition of a reflection followed by a translation parallel to the line of reflection.
questions.explanation: A glide reflection is the composition of a reflection across a line followed by a translation parallel to that same line. It is one of four isometry types in the plane (G-CO.2). The glide reflection has no fixed points and reverses orientation. Footprint patterns when walking illustrate glide reflections — each footprint is a reflected, translated copy of the previous. On Regents, glide reflections appear in composition problems asking students to identify the single transformation equivalent to two successive rigid motions.

questions.prompt: General name for any transformation that preserves both distance and angle measure.
questions.explanation: An isometry is any transformation that preserves all distances and angle measures — equivalently, one that maps every segment to a congruent segment. The four isometries of the plane are: translation, rotation, reflection, and glide reflection (G-CO.2). Because distances are preserved, the image is always congruent to the pre-image. Felix Klein's Erlangen Program (1872) defined geometry itself through the group of isometries that leave a space's properties invariant. On Regents, identifying whether a given transformation is an isometry is a common reasoning question.

questions.prompt: Notation such as (x,y) -> (x+3,y-2) used on Regents items to describe a transformation rule.
questions.explanation: Coordinate notation expresses a transformation as a mapping rule applied to coordinates, e.g., (x,y)→(x+3,y-2) for a translation, or (x,y)→(-y,x) for a 90° counterclockwise rotation about the origin (G-CO.2). NY Geometry Regents items commonly give a rule and ask students to find image coordinates, identify the transformation, or verify congruence. Coordinate notation connects synthetic geometry to the Cartesian plane introduced by René Descartes in La Géométrie (1637), making algebraic analysis of geometric figures possible.

questions.prompt: Resulting figure after a transformation, usually labeled with primes such as A'.
questions.explanation: The image is the figure that results after a transformation is applied to the pre-image. Standard notation uses prime labels (A', B', C'). For an isometry, the image is congruent to the pre-image; for a dilation, the image is similar. On NY Geometry Regents, image coordinates are found by applying the transformation rule to each vertex. When a composition is performed, intermediate images are labeled with double primes (A'', B'', C'') (G-CO.2, G-CO.4).

questions.prompt: Fixed point about which every point of a figure turns the same angle measure.
questions.explanation: The center of rotation is the unique fixed point about which every other point of the figure turns through the rotation angle. To locate it given a pre-image/image pair, construct the perpendicular bisectors of two segments connecting corresponding points — they intersect at the center (G-CO.4). On NY Geometry Regents, the origin is the most common center, but items also test rotations about non-origin points. The rotation angle and center together completely determine a rotation.

questions.prompt: Line across which each point of a figure is mapped to its mirror image at equal distance.
questions.explanation: The line of reflection is the set of all fixed points of a reflection — every point on the line maps to itself. For any corresponding pair (P, P'), the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of PP'. Common Regents lines: x-axis, y-axis, y=x, y=-x, and lines like x=2 (G-CO.2, G-CO.4). To construct a line of reflection given a pre-image and image, connect pairs of corresponding vertices and construct the perpendicular bisectors — both bisectors coincide at the line of reflection.

questions.prompt: Non-rigid transformation that produces a similar figure by stretching or shrinking from a center.
questions.explanation: A dilation is a non-isometry transformation centered at a fixed point that multiplies every distance from the center by a scale factor k (k>0, k≠1). The coordinate rule for a dilation centered at the origin is D_k(x,y)=(kx,ky). Because angles are preserved but lengths are scaled, the image is similar (not congruent) to the pre-image (G-SRT.1). Dilations were implicit in Euclid's theory of proportion (Elements, Book VI, ~300 BCE). On Regents, dilation problems ask students to find scale factors, image coordinates, or the center given a pre-image/image pair.

questions.prompt: Ratio of image length to preimage length under a dilation.
questions.explanation: The scale factor k of a dilation is the ratio of any image length to its corresponding pre-image length: k = image/pre-image. If k>1 the dilation is an enlargement; if 0<k<1 it is a reduction (G-SRT.1). The scale factor is consistent for every pair of corresponding lengths. On NY Geometry Regents, students find k by dividing image side length by pre-image side length. The scale factor also equals the ratio of image perimeter to pre-image perimeter, while areas scale by k².

questions.prompt: Fixed point from which a figure is enlarged or reduced under a dilation.
questions.explanation: The center of dilation is the unique fixed point — every image point lies along the ray from center through its pre-image, at distance k times the original distance. For an origin-centered dilation, the rule is (x,y)→(kx,ky). To find the center given a pre-image/image pair, draw lines through each pair of corresponding vertices — they intersect at the center (G-SRT.1). Regents items frequently ask students to identify the center from a graph.

questions.prompt: Dilation with a scale factor greater than 1.
questions.explanation: An enlargement is a dilation whose scale factor k>1, producing a larger similar image. Every distance from the center of dilation is multiplied by k. On NY Geometry Regents, enlargement problems might state 'a dilation of scale factor 3 centered at the origin' and ask for image coordinates (G-SRT.1). Enlargements preserve angle measures and parallel lines — the image of any segment not through the center is parallel to its pre-image. Perimeter scales by k and area scales by k².

questions.prompt: Dilation with a scale factor between 0 and 1.
questions.explanation: A reduction is a dilation whose scale factor satisfies 0<k<1, producing a smaller similar image. Every length in the image equals k times the corresponding pre-image length. Because angles are preserved, the image is similar to the pre-image (G-SRT.1). On NY Geometry Regents, a common reduction item gives the image and asks for the scale factor, found by dividing any image length by its corresponding pre-image length. Map-making uses reductions: a 1:24,000 scale map applies a reduction factor of 1/24,000.

questions.prompt: Property of a figure that maps onto itself after a rotation of less than 360 degrees about its center.
questions.explanation: A figure has rotational symmetry of order n if it maps onto itself after a rotation of 360°/n about its center. A square has order 4 (rotations of 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°); an equilateral triangle has order 3 (G-CO.3). The minimum rotation angle is 360°/n. On NY Geometry Regents, students determine the order of rotational symmetry for regular polygons and other figures. A figure with order ≥2 always looks the same at multiple orientations.

questions.prompt: Rotational symmetry of order 2 — every 180-degree rotation maps the figure onto itself.
questions.explanation: Point symmetry is rotational symmetry of order exactly 2 — the figure maps onto itself after a 180° rotation about the center point. Every point P of the figure has a corresponding point P' on the opposite side of the center at the same distance (G-CO.3). Parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, and squares all have point symmetry about the intersection of their diagonals. Regular polygons with even numbers of sides have point symmetry.

questions.prompt: Polygon with all sides congruent and all angles congruent — has both line and rotational symmetry.
questions.explanation: A regular polygon has all sides congruent and all interior angles congruent. A regular n-gon has n lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order n (G-CO.3). Interior angle measure = 180(n-2)/n; each central angle = 360°/n. Area = (1/2)ap where a is the apothem and p is the perimeter (G-MG.1). Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE, Books IV and XIII) showed how to construct regular polygons with compass and straightedge, proving the regular pentagon and linking them to the five Platonic solids.

questions.prompt: Direction of the labeled vertices (clockwise or counterclockwise) — preserved by translation and rotation but reversed by reflection.
questions.explanation: Orientation refers to the direction in which vertices of a figure are ordered — clockwise or counterclockwise. Direct isometries (translations and rotations) preserve orientation; opposite isometries (reflections and glide reflections) reverse it (G-CO.2). On NY Geometry Regents, orientation is tested in composition problems: if a figure goes from counterclockwise to clockwise, an odd number of reflections must have been applied. Checking orientation is a quick way to eliminate incorrect transformation identifications.

questions.prompt: Result of performing one transformation followed by another, applied to the image of the first.
questions.explanation: A composition of transformations applies two or more transformations in sequence; the image of each becomes the pre-image of the next. Notation T₂∘T₁ means apply T₁ first, then T₂ (G-CO.2). Two reflections across parallel lines produce a translation; across intersecting lines, a rotation. On NY Geometry Regents, composition problems ask students to find the final image or identify the single equivalent transformation. The order matters — in general T₂∘T₁ ≠ T₁∘T₂.

questions.prompt: Pairs of vertices, one on the preimage and one on the image, that are matched by a transformation.
questions.explanation: Corresponding vertices are matched pairs — one on the pre-image, one on the image — related by the transformation. In △ABC ≅ △DEF, vertex A corresponds to D, B to E, and C to F (G-CO.7). On Regents, the transformation notation establishes correspondence: if T maps A→A', B→B', C→C', the congruence statement is △ABC ≅ △A'B'C'. Misidentifying correspondence is a common error. Corresponding sides and angles are congruent for isometries, proportional for dilations.

questions.prompt: Property of every rigid motion — segment lengths in the preimage equal those in the image.
questions.explanation: Distance preservation means that for any rigid motion, the distance between any two image points equals the distance between the corresponding pre-image points: |A'B'|=|AB|. This is the defining property of all isometries (G-CO.2, G-CO.6). Because distances are preserved, corresponding segments are congruent, so the image and pre-image are congruent figures. On Regents, students cite 'rigid motions preserve distance' as the justification for concluding congruence from a transformation-based proof.

questions.prompt: Property of every rigid motion AND every dilation — angle measures stay the same.
questions.explanation: Every rigid motion preserves angle measure — the angle at any image vertex equals the angle at the corresponding pre-image vertex (G-CO.2). Dilations also preserve angle measure (but not distance), making similarity the resulting relationship. Angle preservation is why both congruent and similar triangles have equal corresponding angles. On NY Geometry Regents, students cite angle preservation to justify congruent or equal angle pairs in transformation proofs.

questions.prompt: Statement such as Triangle ABC maps onto Triangle DEF that names the correspondence between preimage and image vertices.
questions.explanation: A mapping statement expresses a transformation in words, e.g., 'Triangle ABC maps onto Triangle DEF under a 90° rotation about the origin,' establishing vertex correspondence. The correspondence determines the congruence or similarity statement that follows: △ABC ≅ △DEF or △ABC ~ △DEF (G-CO.6, G-SRT.2). On NY Geometry Regents, students write mapping statements to justify proofs and confirm that two figures are congruent or similar. The mapping statement must list vertices in corresponding order.

questions.prompt: Statement at the top of a two-column proof that lists what may be assumed true at the start.
questions.explanation: The given statement opens a two-column proof, listing all information stated in the problem as true. Every subsequent step must trace back to the given statements or previously proved theorems (G-CO.9). On NY Geometry Regents, students lose credit for citing information as given that was not stated in the problem. The given statement establishes the starting point; the logical chain from given to prove is the essence of Euclidean deductive proof, formalized in Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE) as the model for rigorous mathematical argument.

questions.prompt: Statement that names what a two-column proof must establish from the given information.
questions.explanation: The prove statement names the conclusion that a two-column proof must establish through a logical chain of statements and reasons. It appears at the top of the proof alongside the given statement (G-CO.9, G-CO.10). On NY Geometry Regents, 3- and 6-credit proof questions state 'Prove: [conclusion]' and students must reach that exact statement as the final line. A common error is stopping one step short. The prove statement determines which theorems are relevant — students work backward from it to plan the proof strategy.

questions.prompt: Statement that has been proved deductively from postulates and earlier results.
questions.explanation: A theorem is a mathematical statement proved deductively from postulates, definitions, and previously established theorems. Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE) organized hundreds of theorems in logical order from five postulates (G-CO.9, G-CO.10). On NY Geometry Regents, students cite theorems by name in proof Reason columns: 'Alternate Interior Angles Theorem,' 'Base Angles Theorem,' 'Pythagorean Theorem.' A theorem may only be cited after it has been proved or is listed as given. Stating the theorem name alone is sufficient as a reason.

questions.prompt: Statement accepted as true without proof — also called an axiom.
questions.explanation: A postulate (axiom) is a statement accepted as true without proof — the starting assumptions of an axiomatic system. Euclid's five postulates (~300 BCE) include: a straight line can be drawn between any two points, and all right angles are equal (G-CO.9). The Parallel Postulate (through a point not on a line, exactly one parallel exists) is the foundation of Euclidean geometry. On NY Geometry Regents, postulates appear as proof reasons: 'Segment Addition Postulate,' 'Angle Addition Postulate,' 'SAS Postulate.'

questions.prompt: Ordered chain of statements and reasons that moves from given information to the prove statement.
questions.explanation: A logical sequence in a two-column proof is the ordered chain of statements, each supported by a cited reason, that moves from given information to the prove statement without gaps (G-CO.9). Each reason must be a definition, postulate, or previously proved theorem. On NY Geometry Regents, partial credit is awarded per step — a broken logical sequence loses credit at the point of the gap. Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE) established this model: every proposition is proved by a numbered sequence of justified steps from first principles.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent.
questions.explanation: SSS (Side-Side-Side) is a triangle congruence postulate guaranteeing congruence when all three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent (G-CO.8). Euclid proved SSS as Proposition 8 of Elements Book I (~300 BCE). On Regents, SSS is applied in coordinate proofs by using the distance formula to verify three pairs of equal side lengths, then citing SSS as the justification. SSS applies to all triangles — it is the most general congruence criterion and requires no angle information.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of sides and the included angle are congruent.
questions.explanation: SAS (Side-Angle-Side) is a triangle congruence postulate guaranteeing congruence when two pairs of corresponding sides and the included angle between them are congruent (G-CO.8). Included means the angle is formed by the two named sides — citing SSA is invalid. Euclid proved SAS as Proposition 4 of Elements Book I (~300 BCE) using superposition. On NY Geometry Regents, SAS is used in two-column proofs; identifying the included angle correctly is the critical step.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of angles and the included side are congruent.
questions.explanation: ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) is a triangle congruence postulate guaranteeing congruence when two pairs of corresponding angles and the included side between them are congruent (G-CO.8). The side must be between the two named angles. Euclid proved ASA as Proposition 26 of Elements Book I. On Regents, students distinguish ASA from AAS by checking whether the known side is included (between the two angles) — if so, ASA; if not, AAS.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of angles and a non-included side are congruent.
questions.explanation: AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) guarantees triangle congruence when two pairs of corresponding angles and one pair of non-included corresponding sides are congruent (G-CO.8). AAS is a theorem provable from ASA and the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem. A common Regents strategy: use vertical angles or alternate interior angles to establish two angle pairs, then identify a shared or given side to apply AAS. The non-included side can be any side as long as its position matches in both triangles.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle congruence criterion using a pair of hypotenuses and one pair of legs.
questions.explanation: HL (Hypotenuse-Leg) is a right-triangle congruence theorem: two right triangles are congruent when they have congruent hypotenuses and one pair of congruent legs (G-CO.8). HL applies only to right triangles because the right angle is always a given condition. It is provable from SSS using the Pythagorean theorem to find the third side. On NY Geometry Regents, HL is cited in proofs where perpendicular lines establish the right angle and the hypotenuse and one leg are shown congruent.

questions.prompt: Pairs of sides or angles in congruent triangles — the C, P, C, T, C of CPCTC.
questions.explanation: CPCTC — Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent — is cited in the final steps of a triangle congruence proof: once two triangles are proved congruent by SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL, any remaining pair of corresponding sides or angles can be declared congruent by CPCTC (G-CO.7). On NY Geometry Regents, CPCTC is the most commonly cited reason in Part II and Part IV proof questions. It is always applied after — never before — the triangles have been proved congruent.

questions.prompt: Side that lies between two named angles of a triangle.
questions.explanation: An included side is the side that lies between two named angles of a triangle — it has both angle vertices as its endpoints. In ASA congruence, the 'S' refers to this included side (G-CO.8). In △ABC, side BC is included between angles B and C. Identifying the included side is critical to distinguishing ASA from AAS on Regents proof items. The included side is always the one shared by the two known angles; if the known side is not between the two known angles, AAS applies instead.

questions.prompt: One of the two congruent angles opposite the congruent sides of an isosceles triangle.
questions.explanation: Base angles are the two congruent angles opposite the congruent sides of an isosceles triangle. The Isosceles Triangle Theorem (Euclid, Elements Book I Prop. 5, ~300 BCE) states: if two sides of a triangle are congruent, the angles opposite them are congruent (G-CO.10). On NY Geometry Regents, the converse is equally important: if two angles are congruent, the sides opposite them are congruent. Base angle problems frequently involve setting up an equation from equal angle expressions and solving for a variable.

questions.prompt: Segment from a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side.
questions.explanation: A median of a triangle is a segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. The three medians are concurrent at the centroid, which divides each median in a 2:1 ratio from vertex to midpoint (G-CO.10, G-GPE.4). The centroid is the triangle's center of gravity. On NY Geometry Regents, median problems ask students to find the midpoint using the midpoint formula, verify the median, or use the 2:1 centroid ratio. Archimedes (~250 BCE) used centroids in his study of equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles on opposite sides of a transversal and between two lines — congruent when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are on opposite sides of a transversal and between the parallel lines — they are congruent when lines are parallel (Alternate Interior Angles Theorem, G-CO.9). Conversely, if alternate interior angles are congruent, the lines are parallel. Euclid proved this in Elements Book I Proposition 29. On NY Geometry Regents, alternate interior angles appear as reasons in parallel-line proofs and as the mechanism for proving triangles congruent via AAS when parallel lines create congruent angle pairs.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles in matching corners at each intersection of a transversal — congruent when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Corresponding angles are in matching positions at each intersection when a transversal crosses two lines — same side of the transversal, one above and one below each parallel line. When lines are parallel, corresponding angles are congruent (Corresponding Angles Postulate, G-CO.9). The converse also holds. On NY Geometry Regents, corresponding angles are cited in proofs involving parallel lines cut by a transversal and appear in F-shaped diagrams.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles on the same side of a transversal and between two lines — supplementary when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles (co-interior or consecutive interior angles) are on the same side of a transversal and between the parallel lines — they are supplementary (sum to 180°) when lines are parallel (Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem, G-CO.9). On NY Geometry Regents, students set up equations like (3x+10)+(5x-30)=180 when these angles are labeled with expressions. They form a C-shape or U-shape in diagrams. The converse — if same-side interior angles are supplementary, lines are parallel — is also tested.

questions.prompt: Through a point not on a given line, exactly one line can be drawn parallel to the given line.
questions.explanation: The Parallel Postulate (Euclid, Elements Book I, ~300 BCE): through a point not on a given line, exactly one line can be drawn parallel to the given line — known as Playfair's Axiom (1795). This postulate distinguishes Euclidean geometry from non-Euclidean geometries developed by Gauss, Bolyai, and Lobachevsky in the 1820s. On NY Geometry Regents, the parallel postulate underpins all parallel-line theorems used in proofs. G-CO.9 requires students to prove theorems about parallel lines using this foundational axiom.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral proved by showing both pairs of opposite sides are parallel — or both pairs are congruent.
questions.explanation: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Proved properties: opposite sides are congruent, opposite angles are congruent, consecutive angles are supplementary, and diagonals bisect each other (G-CO.11). In a coordinate proof, a quadrilateral is a parallelogram if opposite sides have equal slopes and equal lengths. Proofs typically cite the definition (both pairs of opposite sides parallel) or the theorem that both pairs of opposite sides are congruent. The word comes from Greek parallelo-gramma, meaning bounded by parallel lines.

questions.prompt: Parallelogram proved by showing it has one right angle or congruent diagonals.
questions.explanation: A rectangle is a parallelogram with four right angles. A quadrilateral is proved to be a rectangle by showing it is a parallelogram with one right angle (all four follow), or by showing its diagonals are congruent and bisect each other (G-CO.11). In a coordinate proof, equal diagonal lengths (distance formula) confirm a rectangle. A rectangle's diagonals are congruent but do not bisect at right angles (unlike a square or rhombus). Area = l×w. Every rectangle is a parallelogram, but not every parallelogram is a rectangle.

questions.prompt: Parallelogram proved by showing all four sides congruent or perpendicular diagonals.
questions.explanation: A rhombus is a parallelogram with all four sides congruent. Proved to be a rhombus by showing all four sides equal or by showing diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other (G-CO.11). In a coordinate proof, perpendicular diagonals (slopes that are negative reciprocals) confirm a rhombus. The diagonals bisect the vertex angles. Area = (d₁×d₂)/2. Every square is a rhombus, but not every rhombus is a square.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral that is simultaneously a rectangle and a rhombus.
questions.explanation: A square is simultaneously a rectangle (four right angles) and a rhombus (four congruent sides) — both equilateral and equiangular (G-CO.11). A square's diagonals are congruent (rectangle property) AND perpendicular bisectors of each other (rhombus property). On NY Geometry Regents, a quadrilateral is proved to be a square by showing all four sides congruent and all four angles right. A regular polygon with n=4, the square has four lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 4.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases).
questions.explanation: A trapezoid is a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases); the non-parallel sides are the legs (G-CO.11). An isosceles trapezoid has congruent legs, congruent base angles, and congruent diagonals. The Midsegment Theorem: the segment connecting midpoints of the legs is parallel to both bases with length m=(b₁+b₂)/2. Area = (1/2)(b₁+b₂)h. On NY Geometry Regents, coordinate proofs identify trapezoids by showing exactly one pair of opposite sides with equal slopes.

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion guaranteed when two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent.
questions.explanation: AA (Angle-Angle) Similarity Theorem: two triangles are similar when two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent (G-SRT.3). Because the angle sum of a triangle is 180°, two equal angle pairs force the third pair to be equal. AA is the most efficient similarity criterion — no side lengths are needed. On NY Geometry Regents, AA is cited when parallel lines create congruent corresponding or alternate interior angles, or when a shared angle and vertical angle provide two equal pairs. Euclid proved AA similarity in Elements Book VI Proposition 4 (~300 BCE).

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion using two pairs of proportional sides and a congruent included angle.
questions.explanation: SAS Similarity Theorem: two triangles are similar when two pairs of corresponding sides are proportional and the included angles between them are congruent (G-SRT.5). The included angle requirement is essential — SAS without the included angle does not guarantee similarity. On NY Geometry Regents, SAS similarity is applied when two side ratios are verified equal and the angle between them is given or proved congruent. It appears in multi-step proof questions and problems involving midsegments and parallel-cut lines.

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion using three pairs of proportional sides.
questions.explanation: SSS Similarity Theorem: two triangles are similar when all three pairs of corresponding sides are proportional — ratios a'/a = b'/b = c'/c all equal the same constant k (G-SRT.5). Unlike SSS congruence (equal lengths), SSS similarity requires equal ratios. On NY Geometry Regents, SSS similarity appears when all six side lengths are given and students must verify proportionality or find a missing side. Euclid's Elements Book VI Proposition 5 (~300 BCE) contains the equivalent result. Once similarity is established, corresponding angles are congruent.

questions.prompt: Triangles with congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides — written with the ~ symbol.
questions.explanation: Two triangles are similar (△ABC ~ △DEF) if corresponding angles are congruent and corresponding sides are proportional (G-SRT.2). Criteria are AA, SAS~, and SSS~. Once similarity is established, all corresponding angle pairs are equal and all side ratios equal the scale factor k. On NY Geometry Regents, problems ask students to set up proportions, solve for missing lengths, or write similarity statements with vertices in correct corresponding order. Thales of Miletus (~600 BCE) used similar triangles to measure the height of Egyptian pyramids by comparing shadow lengths.

questions.prompt: Diagram in which all lengths are reduced or enlarged from real measurements by the same ratio.
questions.explanation: A scale drawing is a diagram in which all lengths are proportional to the actual object by a fixed ratio (G-MG.1, G-SRT.2). A blueprint at scale 1 in:8 ft means every inch on paper represents 8 feet in reality. Students find actual lengths by multiplying drawing measurements by the scale. On NY Geometry Regents, scale drawing problems appear in the modeling strand (G-MG) and require unit conversions. Descartes's coordinate geometry (1637) provided the mathematical basis for precise technical drawing.

questions.prompt: Pairs of corresponding sides in similar figures whose ratios are all equal.
questions.explanation: In similar figures, proportional sides means corresponding side lengths form equal ratios: a/a' = b/b' = c/c' = k, where k is the scale factor (G-SRT.2). Setting up correct proportions — matching corresponding sides, not adjacent sides — is critical. On NY Geometry Regents, students write a proportion from a similarity statement, substitute known values, and solve using cross multiplication. Eudoxus of Cnidus (~370 BCE) developed the theory of proportions that Euclid incorporated into Elements Book V.

questions.prompt: Equal product of the means and extremes used to solve a proportion such as a/b = c/d.
questions.explanation: The Cross Products Property: if a/b = c/d, then ad = bc (product of means equals product of extremes). This is the primary algebraic tool for solving proportion equations from similar triangles (G-SRT.5). On NY Geometry Regents, students apply cross products to find missing side lengths: e.g., if 6/x = 9/15, then 9x=90, so x=10. The property follows from multiplying both sides of the proportion by bd. Cross products also appear in segment-length problems involving secants and chords.

questions.prompt: Number x such that a/x = x/b — appears in the altitude-on-hypotenuse relationships.
questions.explanation: The geometric mean of two positive numbers a and b is x=√(ab), where a/x = x/b (G-SRT.4). In a right triangle, the altitude drawn to the hypotenuse creates two smaller triangles each similar to the original. The altitude length equals the geometric mean of the two hypotenuse segments; each leg equals the geometric mean of the adjacent hypotenuse segment and the full hypotenuse. On NY Geometry Regents, geometric mean problems present a right triangle with the altitude drawn and ask for missing segment lengths.

questions.prompt: Common ratio of corresponding side lengths in two similar figures.
questions.explanation: The similarity ratio (scale factor) is the common ratio k of all corresponding side lengths: k = image side / pre-image side (G-SRT.2). Constant for all pairs of corresponding sides. The ratio of perimeters equals k; areas scale by k². On NY Geometry Regents, the similarity ratio is found by dividing any pair of known corresponding sides, then used to find unknown sides by proportion. If k>1 the image is an enlargement; if 0<k<1 a reduction.

questions.prompt: Fixed point from which every point of a figure is stretched or shrunk to produce a similar image.
questions.explanation: The center of dilation is the fixed point from which all image points are located along rays through their pre-image counterparts at distance k times the original. For origin-centered dilations, the rule is (x,y)→(kx,ky) (G-SRT.1). To find the center from a pre-image/image pair, draw lines through each pair of corresponding vertices — they converge at the center. Regents items typically provide the center's coordinates for non-origin centers. Identifying the center from a graph is a recurring exam skill in the similarity unit.

questions.prompt: Scale factor k of a dilation — positive value that multiplies every distance from the center.
questions.explanation: The dilation factor k multiplies every distance from the center to produce the image. If k>1 the image is larger; if 0<k<1 it is smaller. In coordinate notation for origin-centered dilations, (x,y)→(kx,ky) (G-SRT.1). Area scales by k². On NY Geometry Regents, students find k by dividing image length by pre-image length, or find image coordinates by multiplying by k. The dilation factor appears explicitly in Regents transformation problems and is the foundation for all similarity proportion work.

questions.prompt: Property of dilation — the image of any segment not through the center is parallel to the preimage segment.
questions.explanation: Under a dilation, the image of any segment not passing through the center of dilation is parallel to the original segment (G-SRT.1). This follows from the definition: every point moves along a ray through the center by factor k, leaving the direction of the segment unchanged. On NY Geometry Regents, this parallelism is cited in proofs where a dilation establishes the Midsegment Theorem (midsegment parallel to base) or the Side-Splitter Theorem (proportional cuts create a parallel line).

questions.prompt: Rule (x,y) -> (kx,ky) that produces a dilation centered at the origin with scale factor k.
questions.explanation: A coordinate dilation centered at the origin with scale factor k maps every point (x,y) to (kx,ky). For a non-origin center (a,b): (x,y)→(a+k(x-a), b+k(y-b)) (G-SRT.1, G-CO.2). On NY Geometry Regents, students apply the origin-centered rule most often, multiplying each coordinate by k. The coordinate form connects the geometric definition to the Cartesian system introduced by René Descartes (1637), making algebraic verification of geometric properties possible.

questions.prompt: Scale factor greater than 1 used in a dilation that produces a larger image.
questions.explanation: An enlargement factor k>1 means every distance from the center of dilation is multiplied by k, producing a larger similar figure (G-SRT.1). If k=2, every length doubles and area quadruples (k²=4). On NY Geometry Regents, enlargement problems give the pre-image coordinates and scale factor and ask for image coordinates, or give both figures and ask students to calculate k by dividing image by pre-image lengths. Enlargement factors appear in real-world modeling problems (projecting an image, enlarging a blueprint), connecting to G-MG applications.

questions.prompt: Segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.
questions.explanation: The Triangle Midsegment Theorem: the segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length (G-CO.10, G-SRT.4). This is proved using dilations: a dilation of scale factor 1/2 centered at one vertex maps the full triangle to the half-triangle, making the midsegment an image of the base (hence parallel). On NY Geometry Regents, midsegment problems set up equations like midsegment = (1/2)(base) and solve for variables.

questions.prompt: Line parallel to one side of a triangle that intersects the other two sides divides them proportionally.
questions.explanation: The Side-Splitter (Triangle Proportionality) Theorem: a line parallel to one side of a triangle intersecting the other two sides divides those sides proportionally: if DE ∥ BC, then AD/DB = AE/EC (G-SRT.4). Proved using similar triangles created by the parallel line. On NY Geometry Regents, problems set up a proportion from the four segments and solve for an unknown. The converse is also tested: proportional division implies parallelism.

questions.prompt: Bisector of an angle of a triangle divides the opposite side in the ratio of the two adjacent sides.
questions.explanation: The Angle Bisector Theorem: the bisector of an angle of a triangle divides the opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides: BD/DC = AB/AC when BD bisects angle B (G-SRT.4). On NY Geometry Regents, problems give three of the four segment lengths and ask for the fourth by proportion. The theorem follows from similar triangles or the Law of Sines. Euclid proved a related proposition in Elements Book VI Proposition 3 (~300 BCE). Students must not confuse this with the perpendicular bisector.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle segment that creates two smaller triangles similar to the original and to each other.
questions.explanation: When an altitude is drawn to the hypotenuse of a right triangle, it creates two smaller triangles each similar to the original and to each other (G-SRT.4). Three geometric mean relationships follow: altitude = √(p·q) where p and q are the hypotenuse segments; each leg = √(full hypotenuse × adjacent segment). On NY Geometry Regents, students set up these proportions and solve for unknown lengths. The property is a consequence of AA similarity applied to the three right triangles sharing the right-angle vertex.

questions.prompt: Ratio of the perimeters of two similar figures, equal to their similarity ratio.
questions.explanation: The ratio of the perimeters of two similar figures equals the similarity ratio k (G-SRT.2). If two similar triangles have sides in ratio 3:5, perimeters are also in ratio 3:5. This follows because perimeter is a sum of lengths, all scaling by the same k. On NY Geometry Regents, perimeter ratio problems give the similarity ratio and one perimeter and ask for the other: P₂=k·P₁. The area ratio equals k². Students must keep the three scaling tiers distinct: linear k, area k², volume k³.

questions.prompt: Classic indirect-measurement setup that pairs an object and its shadow with a known stick and its shadow.
questions.explanation: The shadow problem is the prototypical indirect measurement application (G-SRT.5, G-MG.1): two vertical objects cast shadows simultaneously, creating two similar triangles (same sun angle). Proportion: height/shadow = height₂/shadow₂. Thales (~600 BCE) used this to measure Egyptian pyramids. On NY Geometry Regents, shadow problems involve a person and flagpole, tree and building, or any two objects with simultaneously measured shadows. Students must verify the triangles are similar (same elevation angle of the sun) before writing a proportion.

questions.prompt: Ratio printed on a map such as 1 in : 50 mi that translates map distance to real distance.
questions.explanation: A map scale (e.g., 1 in:50 mi) translates distances on a map to real-world distances (G-MG.1). Students find actual distances by multiplying map measurements by the scale, or map distances by dividing actual by scale. Unit conversions are often required. On NY Geometry Regents, map scale problems appear in the modeling strand and require writing and solving a proportion. The mathematical basis is similarity: a map is a scaled-down similar representation of actual terrain.

questions.prompt: Ratio that relates a model car, building, or train to its real-world counterpart.
questions.explanation: A model scale relates dimensions of a physical model to the actual object's dimensions (G-MG.1). If a model car is at scale 1:24, every model measurement is 1/24 of the real length, and volume is (1/24)³ of the actual. On NY Geometry Regents, model scale problems ask students to find actual dimensions from model dimensions using proportions, often requiring the square or cube of the scale factor for area or volume questions. The scale factor connects directly to G-SRT.2's treatment of similarity ratios.

questions.prompt: Ratio of the areas of two similar figures — equal to the square of the similarity ratio.
questions.explanation: The ratio of the areas of two similar figures equals the square of the similarity ratio: A₂/A₁ = k² (G-SRT.2). If corresponding sides are in ratio 2:5, areas are in ratio 4:25. This follows because area involves two linear dimensions, each scaled by k. On NY Geometry Regents, problems give both side lengths and one area and ask for the other: A₂=k²·A₁. A common error is using k instead of k² for area — the square must not be forgotten.

questions.prompt: Relationship a^2 + b^2 = c^2 connecting the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle.
questions.explanation: The Pythagorean Theorem states that in a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c, a²+b²=c² (G-SRT.8). Pythagoras of Samos (~500 BCE) gave a general proof, but the relationship was known to Babylonians (Plimpton 322, ~1800 BCE) and Egyptians. Over 370 distinct proofs exist, including Euclid's (Elements Book I, Prop. 47) and President James Garfield's 1876 trapezoid proof. On NY Geometry Regents, it appears in nearly every unit: finding side lengths, verifying right angles, finding diagonals, and computing coordinate distances.

questions.prompt: Set of three whole numbers, such as 3-4-5 or 5-12-13, that satisfy a^2 + b^2 = c^2.
questions.explanation: A Pythagorean triple is a set of three positive integers satisfying a²+b²=c², such as 3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17, and 7-24-25 (G-SRT.8). Multiples are also triples: 6-8-10, 9-12-15. General formula: a=m²-n², b=2mn, c=m²+n² for integers m>n>0. On NY Geometry Regents, Pythagorean triples appear in problems designed for clean integer answers — recognizing 3-4-5 or 5-12-13 immediately gives the answer without a calculator. Memorizing the most common triples saves significant time on the exam.

questions.prompt: Angle of exactly 90 degrees — marked with a small square at the vertex.
questions.explanation: A right angle measures exactly 90° and is marked with a small square symbol in diagrams (G-CO.9). Right angles are foundational: the Pythagorean theorem applies only to right triangles; trig ratios are defined in terms of right-triangle sides; perpendicular lines form right angles. The converse of the Pythagorean theorem (if a²+b²=c², the triangle is a right triangle) allows students to verify a right angle from given side lengths — a common proof technique on NY Geometry Regents.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to opposite over hypotenuse.
questions.explanation: The sine of an acute angle in a right triangle equals opposite over hypotenuse: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). Mnemonic: SOH. On NY Geometry Regents, sine is used to find an unknown side (multiply both sides by hyp) or unknown angle (use sin⁻¹). Exact values: sin 30°=1/2, sin 45°=√2/2, sin 60°=√3/2. The sine function was first systematically tabulated by Indian mathematician Aryabhata (~499 CE) and later extended by Arab mathematicians in the 9th century.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to adjacent over hypotenuse.
questions.explanation: The cosine of an acute angle in a right triangle equals adjacent over hypotenuse: cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). Mnemonic: CAH. Exact values: cos 30°=√3/2, cos 45°=√2/2, cos 60°=1/2. On NY Geometry Regents, cosine is used to find an unknown adjacent side or hypotenuse. The co-function identity sin θ = cos(90°-θ) means cosine is literally the sine of the complement — a recurring Regents multiple-choice check.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to opposite over adjacent.
questions.explanation: The tangent of an acute angle in a right triangle equals opposite over adjacent: tan θ = opposite/adjacent (G-SRT.6). Mnemonic: TOA. Tangent does not involve the hypotenuse. Exact values: tan 30°=√3/3, tan 45°=1, tan 60°=√3. On NY Geometry Regents, tangent is especially useful in angle-of-elevation and depression problems where the horizontal distance and vertical height are the known or unknown quantities. The word 'tangent' comes from Latin tangens ('touching'), originally a line tangent to a unit circle.

questions.prompt: Side of a right triangle directly across from the acute angle being referenced.
questions.explanation: The opposite side is the leg directly across the triangle from the reference acute angle — not connected to the angle's vertex (G-SRT.6). Used in sine: sin θ = opp/hyp, and tangent: tan θ = opp/adj. On NY Geometry Regents, students first identify the reference angle, then label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse before choosing the correct trig ratio. The 'opposite' label changes when the reference angle changes — a leg opposite one acute angle is adjacent to the other.

questions.prompt: Leg of a right triangle that, together with the hypotenuse, forms the acute angle being referenced.
questions.explanation: The adjacent side is the leg that forms one side of the reference acute angle (together with the hypotenuse) — next to the angle but not the hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). Used in cosine: cos θ = adj/hyp, and tangent: tan θ = opp/adj. On NY Geometry Regents, students must not confuse the adjacent leg with the hypotenuse — both share the vertex of the reference angle, but the hypotenuse is opposite the right angle. If the reference angle changes, adjacent and opposite labels swap.

questions.prompt: Special right triangle with side ratio 1 : sqrt(3) : 2.
questions.explanation: A 30-60-90 triangle has sides in ratio 1:√3:2, opposite the 30°, 60°, and 90° angles respectively (G-SRT.8). If the short leg (opposite 30°) is n, the long leg is n√3 and the hypotenuse is 2n. These ratios come from cutting an equilateral triangle in half along an altitude. On NY Geometry Regents, 30-60-90 triangles appear in problems with exact radical answers: hypotenuse 10 → short leg 5, long leg 5√3. Students recall the ratio from the exact values sin 30°=1/2, cos 30°=√3/2.

questions.prompt: Special right triangle with side ratio 1 : 1 : sqrt(2).
questions.explanation: A 45-45-90 isosceles right triangle has legs in ratio 1:1 and hypotenuse √2 (G-SRT.8). If each leg is n, the hypotenuse is n√2. From the Pythagorean theorem: n²+n²=c² gives c=n√2. A square's diagonal creates two 45-45-90 triangles. A square with side s has diagonal s√2. Students find missing sides: if hypotenuse is given, divide by √2 (multiply by √2/2). Exact values sin 45°=cos 45°=√2/2 and tan 45°=1 confirm these ratios.

questions.prompt: Exact side length expressed with a square-root symbol, such as 5*sqrt(2), required by Regents free-response.
questions.explanation: A radical side length is an exact length expressed using a square-root symbol (e.g., 3√2 or 5√3) rather than a decimal approximation (G-SRT.8). Radical lengths arise from the Pythagorean theorem and special right triangles. On NY Geometry Regents, exact radical form is required for answers involving surds; decimals lose precision. To simplify: √50=√(25·2)=5√2. Add/subtract like radicals: 3√2+5√2=8√2. Rationalize denominators: 1/√2=√2/2. The reference sheet reminds students to leave answers in simplest radical form.

questions.prompt: Trig value such as sin 60 = sqrt(3)/2 expressed without a calculator decimal.
questions.explanation: Exact trig values are ratios expressed without a calculator: sin 30°=1/2, cos 45°=√2/2, tan 60°=√3 (G-SRT.6). These values come from the 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangles and the unit circle. On NY Geometry Regents, multiple-choice items often require recognizing an exact value. Students who memorize the special-angle values for 30°, 45°, 60° answer these quickly. The complementary identity sin θ=cos(90°-θ) extends the list: sin 60°=cos 30°=√3/2.

questions.prompt: Right triangle with two congruent legs — also called a 45-45-90 triangle.
questions.explanation: An isosceles right triangle has two congruent legs meeting at the right angle, making both acute angles 45° — the 45-45-90 triangle (G-SRT.8). The diagonal of a square creates two isosceles right triangles. If each leg is n, the hypotenuse is n√2. On NY Geometry Regents, isosceles right triangle problems arise in square diagonal problems and exact-radical-length problems. The equal legs follow from the Isosceles Triangle Theorem; the 90° angle must be given or proved.

questions.prompt: Angle measured upward from a horizontal line of sight to an object above.
questions.explanation: The angle of elevation is measured upward from a horizontal line of sight to an object above the observer (G-SRT.8, G-MG.1). Always measured from horizontal, not vertical. On NY Geometry Regents, problems describe looking up at a building and give the horizontal distance and angle, asking for height — solved with tan(elevation)=height/distance. The angle of elevation from point A to point B equals the angle of depression from B to A.

questions.prompt: Angle measured downward from a horizontal line of sight to an object below.
questions.explanation: The angle of depression is measured downward from a horizontal line of sight to an object below the observer (G-SRT.8, G-MG.1). Measured from horizontal. The angle of depression equals the alternate interior angle of elevation from the ground (parallel horizontal lines). On NY Geometry Regents, depression problems give a height and angle and ask for horizontal distance: distance=height/tan(depression). Students must draw an auxiliary horizontal line at the observer's level to correctly identify the right triangle.

questions.prompt: Imaginary straight line from an observer's eye to the object being viewed.
questions.explanation: The line of sight is the straight line from an observer's eye to the viewed object — it forms the hypotenuse of the right triangle in angle-of-elevation and depression problems (G-SRT.8, G-MG.1). The horizontal and vertical legs complete the triangle. On NY Geometry Regents, diagrams label the line of sight and the angle it makes with the horizontal. Careful diagram reading is essential — the line of sight is not always the hypotenuse in every orientation.

questions.prompt: Direction angle measured clockwise from due north, used in navigation problems.
questions.explanation: A bearing is a direction angle measured clockwise from due north, used in navigation problems (G-MG.1, G-SRT.8). For example, bearing 070° means 70° clockwise from north (northeast). On NY Geometry Regents, bearing problems require sketching a diagram from the verbal description (north = up, east = right), identifying the right triangle, and applying the appropriate trig ratio or Pythagorean theorem. Both trig computation and careful diagram construction from written specifications are tested.

questions.prompt: Word problem that asks for a vertical distance using a trig ratio and a measured angle.
questions.explanation: A height problem asks for a vertical distance (height of a building, tree, cliff) using trig ratios, the Pythagorean theorem, or similar triangles (G-SRT.8, G-MG.1). Standard setup: known horizontal distance and angle of elevation → height = distance × tan(angle). On NY Geometry Regents, height problems test students' ability to extract and label the right triangle from a real-world scenario, choose the correct trig ratio, and solve to the specified precision (typically nearest tenth).

questions.prompt: Calculator function sin^-1 that returns the angle whose sine is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse sine function sin⁻¹ (arcsin) returns the angle whose sine is a given value: if sin θ=x, then θ=sin⁻¹(x) (G-SRT.7). On a calculator, sin⁻¹(0.5)=30°. Used when the opposite side and hypotenuse are known and the angle is sought. On NY Geometry Regents, students use sin⁻¹ to find a missing angle, rounding as specified. The calculator must be in DEGREE mode before evaluating — a critical check before every trig computation.

questions.prompt: Calculator function cos^-1 that returns the angle whose cosine is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse cosine function cos⁻¹ (arccos) returns the angle whose cosine is a given value: if cos θ=x, then θ=cos⁻¹(x) (G-SRT.7). On a calculator, cos⁻¹(0.5)=60°. Applied when the adjacent leg and hypotenuse are known and the angle is needed. On NY Geometry Regents, inverse trig functions require DEGREE mode. The co-function identity cos⁻¹(x)=90°-sin⁻¹(x) provides a useful cross-check.

questions.prompt: Calculator function tan^-1 that returns the angle whose tangent is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse tangent function tan⁻¹ (arctan) returns the angle whose tangent is a given value: if tan θ=x, then θ=tan⁻¹(x) (G-SRT.7). On a calculator, tan⁻¹(1)=45°. Used when both legs are known and the angle is needed — hypotenuse is irrelevant. On NY Geometry Regents, tan⁻¹ is the most frequently used inverse trig function because angle-of-elevation problems commonly give horizontal distance and height (both legs).

questions.prompt: Regents rule such as round to the nearest tenth or nearest degree applied to a trig answer.
questions.explanation: The NY Geometry Regents specifies a rounding convention in each problem — typically 'to the nearest tenth,' 'to the nearest degree,' or 'to the nearest hundredth.' Failing to round to the correct precision loses credit even if the method is correct (G-SRT.8). On multi-step problems, maintain full calculator precision through intermediate steps and round only the final answer. Rounding too early introduces accumulated error that can shift the answer outside the rubric's acceptable range.

questions.prompt: Setting (DEGREE vs RADIAN) that must match the unit of the angle for trig calculations to be correct.
questions.explanation: The calculator mode — DEGREE versus RADIAN — must match the unit of the angle in the problem (G-SRT.8). On NY Geometry Regents, all angles are in degrees, so the calculator must always be in DEGREE mode. In RADIAN mode, sin(30)≈-0.988 instead of sin(30°)=0.5. On the TI-84, DEGREE mode is set via the MODE key. One full revolution = 360° = 2π radians — the degree-radian conversion is also tested in the circles unit (G-C.5).

questions.prompt: Segment from the center of a circle to any point on the circle.
questions.explanation: A radius is a segment from the center of a circle to any point on the circle; all radii are congruent (G-C.2). The radius is half the diameter: r=d/2. In the standard equation (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r², r is the square root of the constant on the right. On NY Geometry Regents, radius problems include finding r from the equation, from a diameter, from a tangent-radius relationship (radius ⊥ tangent), or from sector area/arc length formulas. Euclid defined a circle in Elements Book I (~300 BCE) as all points equidistant from a center.

questions.prompt: Chord that passes through the center of a circle — equal in length to twice the radius.
questions.explanation: A diameter is a chord through the center of a circle; it equals twice the radius: d=2r and is the longest chord (G-C.2). Thales' Theorem (Thales of Miletus, ~600 BCE): an angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle — proved by Euclid in Elements Book III Prop. 31. On NY Geometry Regents, diameter problems involve finding d from r (or vice versa), identifying diameters in diagrams, and applying the Thales result to establish right angles in proofs.

questions.prompt: Segment whose endpoints both lie on a circle.
questions.explanation: A chord is a segment whose two endpoints both lie on a circle (G-C.2). A diameter is the special chord through the center. The Intersecting Chords Theorem: when two chords cross inside a circle, AE·EC=BE·ED. The perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects it. On NY Geometry Regents, chord problems use the intersecting-chords product or the perpendicular-bisector property to locate the center. These relationships are foundational to G-C.2.

questions.prompt: Line that intersects a circle at exactly two points.
questions.explanation: A secant is a line intersecting a circle at exactly two points (G-C.2). For two secants from external point P: angle = (far arc - near arc)/2; segments satisfy: external·whole = external·whole. On NY Geometry Regents, secant problems require setting up these equations to find arc measures or segment lengths. A chord is a secant segment with both endpoints on the circle; a secant extends beyond. The term comes from Latin secare ('to cut').

questions.prompt: Line that touches a circle at exactly one point and is perpendicular to the radius at that point.
questions.explanation: A tangent to a circle is a line touching the circle at exactly one point (point of tangency). Fundamental theorem (G-C.2): a tangent is perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of tangency. Two tangent segments from the same external point are congruent. On NY Geometry Regents, tangent properties are cited in proofs: establishing a right angle (tangent ⊥ radius) and equal tangent lengths. The angle formed by a tangent and chord equals half the intercepted arc. Archimedes (~250 BCE) used tangent lines extensively in his work on spirals.

questions.prompt: Angle whose vertex is at the center of a circle — measure equals the measure of its intercepted arc.
questions.explanation: A central angle has its vertex at the center of a circle; its measure equals the measure of its intercepted arc (G-C.2). If a central angle is 80°, it intercepts an 80° arc. All non-overlapping central angles around a point sum to 360°. On NY Geometry Regents, central angles drive arc-measure problems (arc = central angle) and sector-area problems: sector area = (central angle/360)·πr². Euclid defined the central angle in Elements Book III (~300 BCE).

questions.prompt: Angle whose vertex lies on a circle — measure equals half its intercepted arc.
questions.explanation: An inscribed angle has its vertex on the circle and sides as chords; its measure equals half the intercepted arc (Inscribed Angle Theorem, G-C.2). The intercepted arc = 2 × inscribed angle. All inscribed angles intercepting the same arc are congruent. An inscribed angle intercepting a semicircle measures 90° (Thales' Theorem, ~600 BCE). On NY Geometry Regents, problems set up the equation angle=(arc)/2. Confusing central angle (= arc) with inscribed angle (= half arc) is the top circle-unit error.

questions.prompt: Arc of a circle that measures less than 180 degrees.
questions.explanation: A minor arc measures less than 180° — the shorter arc between two points (G-C.2). Its measure equals the intercepted central angle. Named with two letters: arc AB. The Arc Addition Postulate: adjacent arc measures add: arc ACB = arc AC + arc CB. On NY Geometry Regents, arc measure problems involve central angles, inscribed angles, and arc addition. Distinguishing minor arc (labeled with 2 letters) from major arc (3 letters) is a standard diagram-reading skill.

questions.prompt: Arc of a circle that measures more than 180 degrees.
questions.explanation: A major arc measures more than 180° — the longer arc between two points (G-C.2). Its measure = 360° minus the minor arc between the same endpoints. Named with three letters (two endpoints plus an intermediate point). On NY Geometry Regents, major arc problems appear in inscribed angle and tangent-chord angle problems where the larger arc is intercepted. Mastering the 360° total and the minor/major arc relationship is essential for all circle-angle calculations.

questions.prompt: Arc that measures exactly 180 degrees — endpoints are a diameter.
questions.explanation: A semicircle is the arc measuring exactly 180°, with endpoints at the ends of a diameter (G-C.2). By Thales' Theorem (Thales of Miletus, ~600 BCE; proved in Euclid's Elements Book III Prop. 31), any inscribed angle intercepting a semicircle is a right angle. On NY Geometry Regents, this theorem is used in proofs where a diameter is drawn and a point on the circle connects to both endpoints, forming a right angle at that point. Semicircle arc length = πr; area = πr²/2.

questions.prompt: Segment from an external point to the point of tangency on a circle.
questions.explanation: A tangent segment runs from an external point to the point of tangency. The Two-Tangent Theorem (G-C.2): two tangent segments from the same external point to the same circle are congruent — proved by HL congruence on the two right triangles formed (tangent ⊥ radius). On NY Geometry Regents, this theorem appears in circumscribed-triangle problems where equal tangent-segment lengths are used to find perimeters by setting up algebraic equations.

questions.prompt: Segment from an external point through a circle, intersecting it at two points.
questions.explanation: A secant segment runs from an external point through the circle. The external secant segment is the portion from the external point to the near intersection. Power of a Point for two external secants from P: PA·PB = PC·PD (G-C.2). On NY Geometry Regents, students set up this product equation and solve for an unknown segment, correctly identifying the external portion and the whole segment for each secant.

questions.prompt: Pair of chords meeting inside a circle so that the products of their segment lengths are equal.
questions.explanation: When two chords intersect inside a circle at point E: AE·EC = BE·ED (Intersecting Chords Theorem / Power of a Point, G-C.2). Proved using similar triangles: △AEB ~ △DEC (AA: vertical angles and equal inscribed angles). On NY Geometry Regents, chord-product problems give three of four segment lengths and ask for the fourth: set up the equation and solve. Correctly identifying the two pairs of segments is the critical first step.

questions.prompt: Pair of secants drawn from a common external point — used in the outside-inside product rule.
questions.explanation: When two secants are drawn from external point P: angle P = (far arc - near arc)/2; segments satisfy external·whole = external·whole (G-C.2). For example, external segments 4 and 6, wholes 9 and x: 4·9=6·x, so x=6. On NY Geometry Regents, external-secant problems require identifying far arc (larger) and near arc (smaller), or the external segment and full secant length. Both the angle formula and segment product may appear in the same multi-step item.

questions.prompt: Theorem that the product of segment lengths from a fixed point through a circle is constant regardless of the line chosen.
questions.explanation: The Power of a Point theorem unifies chord, secant, and tangent relationships for a fixed point relative to a circle (G-C.2). Inside: (chord segment 1)·(chord segment 2) = same for any other chord through that point. Outside: (external)·(whole secant) = same for any secant = (tangent segment)². On NY Geometry Regents, each sub-case is tested: students set up the correct product based on point position (inside or outside the circle). The theorem was systematized by Jacob Steiner in the 19th century.

questions.prompt: Point (h, k) read from a circle equation in standard form.
questions.explanation: The center (h,k) is the fixed interior point equidistant from all boundary points, identified from the standard equation (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r² by reading h and k (negating the signs inside the parentheses) (G-GPE.1). From general form x²+y²+Dx+Ey+F=0, the center is found by completing the square on both x and y terms. On NY Geometry Regents, center-finding from an equation is standard. The center is also found geometrically as the intersection of two perpendicular bisectors of chords.

questions.prompt: Form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 obtained by completing the square on x and y.
questions.explanation: Standard form (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r² is obtained from general form x²+y²+Dx+Ey+F=0 by completing the square on x and y groups (G-GPE.1). On NY Geometry Regents, completing-the-square problems give general form and ask for center and radius. Steps: group x-terms and y-terms, add (D/2)² and (E/2)² to both sides, factor as perfect squares, read center and radius. This algebraic technique was developed by al-Khwarizmi (~820 CE) for solving quadratics.

questions.prompt: Algebraic technique used to rewrite x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 in standard circle form.
questions.explanation: Completing the square rewrites x²+bx as (x+b/2)²-(b/2)², converting general circle equation to standard form (G-GPE.1). Applied to both x and y terms of x²+y²+Dx+Ey+F=0 to produce (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r². Steps: add (D/2)² and (E/2)² to both sides, factor left side as perfect squares, subtract F from both sides. If the result is negative or zero, the equation represents no real circle or a single point. On NY Geometry Regents, this is a standard Part II or III skill.

questions.prompt: Length r read directly from a circle equation in the form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2.
questions.explanation: The coordinate radius r is found from (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r² as the square root of the constant on the right (G-GPE.1). If (x-2)²+(y+3)²=49, then r²=49 so r=7. After completing the square from general form, r² is the combined constant. On NY Geometry Regents, students often find r, then verify by substituting a known point. The radius also determines area (πr²) and circumference (2πr), connecting coordinate geometry to measurement formulas.

questions.prompt: Region of a circle bounded by two radii and an arc — equal to (theta/360) * pi * r^2.
questions.explanation: The area of a sector (region bounded by two radii and an arc) is A=(θ/360)·πr², where θ is the central angle in degrees (G-C.5). For a 90° central angle in a circle of radius 6: A=(90/360)·π·36=9π. On NY Geometry Regents, sector area problems give the radius and central angle and ask for exact area (in terms of π) or decimal approximation. Sector area is also related to arc length: A=(1/2)·r·s where s is arc length.

questions.prompt: Length along a circle's edge between two endpoints — equal to (theta/360) * 2 * pi * r.
questions.explanation: Arc length is the distance along a circle's curved edge: L=(θ/360)·2πr, where θ is the central angle in degrees (G-C.5). For a 120° arc in a circle of radius 9: L=(120/360)·18π=6π. On NY Geometry Regents, arc length problems give the radius and central angle; leave answers in terms of π unless a decimal is specified. In radian measure, arc length simplifies to s=rθ — also tested as part of G-C.5.

questions.prompt: Distance around a circle — equal to 2 * pi * r.
questions.explanation: The circumference is the distance around a circle: C=2πr=πd (G-C.5). For r=5, C=10π≈31.4. On NY Geometry Regents, circumference appears in arc length problems (arc = fraction of circumference) and in problems about distance a wheel travels per revolution. Archimedes (~250 BCE) bounded π between 223/71 and 22/7 using inscribed and circumscribed 96-gons. William Jones introduced the symbol π in 1706.

questions.prompt: Region enclosed by a circle — equal to pi * r^2.
questions.explanation: The area enclosed by a circle is A=πr² (G-C.5, G-MG.1). For r=4, A=16π≈50.3. On NY Geometry Regents, area problems require identifying r, substituting into A=πr², and expressing the answer in terms of π (exact) or as a decimal. Archimedes (~250 BCE) proved A=πr² in Measurement of a Circle using the method of exhaustion. The sector area formula A_sector=(θ/360)πr² partitions the full circle area.

questions.prompt: Angle measure based on the ratio of arc length to radius — full circle equals 2 * pi radians.
questions.explanation: Radian measure expresses angle size as arc length divided by radius: θ (rad)=s/r, so s=rθ (G-C.5). One full revolution = 2π radians = 360°. Conversion: degrees × π/180 = radians; radians × 180/π = degrees. Common conversions: 90°=π/2, 180°=π, 270°=3π/2. On NY Geometry Regents, radian measure appears in arc length s=rθ and in degree-radian conversions. The name 'radian' was coined in 1873 by James Thomson; Roger Cotes (~1714) developed the concept.

questions.prompt: Formula d = sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2) for the length of a segment on the coordinate plane.
questions.explanation: The distance formula d=√((x₂-x₁)²+(y₂-y₁)²) gives the length of a coordinate-plane segment, derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the horizontal and vertical legs of a right triangle (G-GPE.7). René Descartes's analytic geometry (La Géométrie, 1637) made this formula possible. On NY Geometry Regents, the distance formula is used to verify side lengths in coordinate proofs of quadrilaterals, find the radius from center to boundary point, and compute diagonal lengths.

questions.prompt: Formula ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2) for the midpoint of a coordinate segment.
questions.explanation: The midpoint formula M=((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2) gives the point halfway between two endpoints (G-GPE.6, G-GPE.7). On NY Geometry Regents, it appears in problems finding midpoints of segments, in coordinate proofs verifying that diagonals bisect each other (both diagonals have the same midpoint), and in constructions of perpendicular bisectors. Descartes's 1637 coordinate system made midpoint calculation algebraic; prior to that, midpoints were found with compass-and-straightedge construction.

questions.prompt: Rule used to find an unknown endpoint of a segment when its midpoint and the other endpoint are known.
questions.explanation: The endpoint formula is derived by rearranging the midpoint formula: if midpoint M and one endpoint are known, the other endpoint = 2·M - known endpoint (G-GPE.6). For example, midpoint (3,5) and endpoint (1,2): other endpoint = (2·3-1, 2·5-2)=(5,8). On NY Geometry Regents, endpoint problems give the midpoint and one endpoint and ask for the other. The centroid formula (average of three vertices) is a related application requiring back-calculation of a missing vertex.

questions.prompt: Formula that locates the point dividing a coordinate segment in a given ratio such as 2:3.
questions.explanation: The partition (section) formula finds the point dividing a segment from (x₁,y₁) to (x₂,y₂) in ratio m:n from the first endpoint: P=(x₁+m/(m+n)·(x₂-x₁), y₁+m/(m+n)·(y₂-y₁)) (G-GPE.6). For a 1:3 partition, the point is 1/4 of the way from first to second endpoint. On NY Geometry Regents, partition problems specify a ratio and ask for coordinates. The midpoint formula is the 1:1 special case. Medians of a triangle divide each other in 2:1 ratio from vertex to midpoint.

questions.prompt: Two-dimensional grid formed by perpendicular x- and y-axes used to plot points.
questions.explanation: The coordinate plane is the two-dimensional grid formed by perpendicular x- and y-axes intersecting at the origin (0,0), introduced by René Descartes in La Géométrie (1637) — the Cartesian plane (G-CO.2, G-GPE). It represents every geometric point as an ordered pair (x,y) and every geometric relationship as an algebraic equation. Pierre de Fermat independently developed a similar system at the same time. The Cartesian plane unified algebra and geometry, making analytic geometry possible.

questions.prompt: Formula m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for the slope of a non-vertical line.
questions.explanation: The slope formula m=(y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁) gives the rate of vertical change per unit of horizontal change for a non-vertical line (G-GPE.5). Positive slope rises left to right; negative falls; zero is horizontal; undefined is vertical. On NY Geometry Regents, slope is central to coordinate proofs of parallel lines (equal slopes), perpendicular lines (negative-reciprocal slopes), and writing line equations. Descartes's 1637 system implicitly used slope; the modern formula was formalized with the development of calculus (Newton and Leibniz, 1680s).

questions.prompt: Two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are this — equal.
questions.explanation: Two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal (G-GPE.5). Vertical lines are parallel without defined slopes. On NY Geometry Regents, the equal-slope criterion is the primary tool in coordinate proofs of parallelograms: show both pairs of opposite sides have equal slopes. A common error is computing slopes correctly but comparing the wrong pairs. The converse — equal slopes implies parallel — is equally important for writing equations of parallel lines.

questions.prompt: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if the product of their slopes is -1.
questions.explanation: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals: m₁·m₂=-1 (G-GPE.5). A horizontal line (slope 0) is perpendicular to a vertical line (undefined slope). On NY Geometry Regents, perpendicular slopes identify right angles in coordinate proofs (proving rectangles, altitudes, perpendicular bisectors). Finding a negative reciprocal: if m=3/4, the perpendicular slope is -4/3. Forgetting to negate when taking the reciprocal is a very common exam error.

questions.prompt: Slope of a vertical line — denominator x2 - x1 equals zero.
questions.explanation: A vertical line has undefined slope because x₁=x₂ makes the denominator of (y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁) equal to zero — division by zero is undefined (G-GPE.5). The equation of a vertical line is x=a. On NY Geometry Regents, vertical lines appear in coordinate proofs where a segment is proved vertical. Two vertical lines are parallel; a vertical line is perpendicular to any horizontal line. Students must state 'undefined slope' rather than writing a ratio with zero in the denominator.

questions.prompt: Slope of a horizontal line — numerator y2 - y1 equals zero.
questions.explanation: A horizontal line has zero slope because y₁=y₂ makes the numerator of (y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁) equal to zero (G-GPE.5). The equation is y=b. On NY Geometry Regents, horizontal lines appear when proving segments are horizontal in coordinate proofs. Two horizontal lines are parallel; a horizontal is perpendicular to any vertical line. Students must clearly distinguish zero slope (horizontal, defined) from undefined slope (vertical). Zero slope means no vertical change.

questions.prompt: Proof that uses coordinates and algebraic formulas to verify a geometric property.
questions.explanation: A coordinate proof uses coordinates and algebraic formulas (distance, midpoint, slope) to verify geometric properties (G-GPE.4). To set up a coordinate proof, place the figure strategically — one vertex at the origin simplifies calculations. Compute relevant quantities and cite the algebraic results as evidence. On NY Geometry Regents Part III and IV, students compute slopes for parallelism/perpendicularity, distances for side and diagonal lengths, and midpoints for bisection. Descartes's 1637 analytic geometry made this proof method possible.

questions.prompt: Result of a coordinate proof that shows a quadrilateral is a parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, or square based on side and diagonal evidence.
questions.explanation: A classified quadrilateral is the result of a coordinate proof identifying a four-sided figure as a specific type — parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, square, or trapezoid — based on slopes, distances, and midpoints (G-GPE.4). The classification hierarchy: every square is both a rectangle and a rhombus; every rectangle and rhombus is a parallelogram. On NY Geometry Regents, the prompt states 'prove that ABCD is a rectangle' and students must show the required combination of properties with full calculation evidence.

questions.prompt: Coordinate-proof evidence such as the diagonals bisect each other or are perpendicular.
questions.explanation: Diagonal properties are key classification evidence in coordinate proofs (G-GPE.4). A parallelogram's diagonals bisect each other (same midpoint). A rectangle's diagonals are congruent and bisect each other. A rhombus's diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other. A square's diagonals are both congruent AND perpendicular bisectors. On NY Geometry Regents, proving a rectangle uses congruent diagonals (distance formula) combined with bisection (midpoint formula showing both diagonals share the same midpoint).

questions.prompt: Use of the distance formula on each side of a quadrilateral to argue for a specific classification.
questions.explanation: Side length evidence uses the distance formula to compute the lengths of a quadrilateral's sides, establishing congruence or inequality (G-GPE.4, G-GPE.7). To prove a rhombus: show all four sides equal. On NY Geometry Regents, side length computation is paired with slope computation: sides with equal slopes are parallel, sides with equal lengths are congruent. Students must label which sides are being compared and state the conclusion clearly for full credit.

questions.prompt: Use of slopes of opposite or adjacent sides to argue parallelism or perpendicularity in a coordinate proof.
questions.explanation: Slope evidence uses the slope formula to establish parallelism (equal slopes) or perpendicularity (negative-reciprocal slopes) between sides (G-GPE.4, G-GPE.5).
